name: Ayllón and Maderuelo Loop
description: This moderate 28.6-mile road cycling route from Ayllón offers diverse landscapes, including the Natural Park of the Hoces del Río Riaza.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Ayllón traverse a diverse landscape within the Central System mountain range, offering varied topography for cyclists. The region features rolling hills, challenging climbs, and scenic routes through ancient forests and dramatic river gorges. This area is characterized by the Ayllón Massif, providing a blend of mountainous terrain and picturesque valleys. Proximity to natural parks like the Hoces of the Riaza River and the Hoces of the Duratón River adds unique geological formations and rich biodiversity to the cycling experience. Best road cycling routes around Ayllón The most popular road cycling route is Ayllón and Maderuelo Loop, a 28.6 miles (46.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 55 minutes to complete, offering moderate climbs through the scenic countryside. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Ayllón and Riaza River Meanders Loop, a difficult 58.7 miles (94.4 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain and views of the impressive gorges carved by the Riaza River. Local road cyclists also love the Medieval Gate of Ayllón – San Bartolomé, Campisábalos loop from Ayllón, a 55.5 miles (89.4 km) trail leading through varied terrain with historical landmarks, often completed in about 4 hours 1 minute. Road cycling around Ayllón is defined by the Ayllón Massif, river gorges, and ancient beech forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to difficult routes with substantial elevation. The routes in Ayllón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 250 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ayllón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Ayllón?
A: There are over 18 road cycling routes around Ayllón, offering a diverse range of experiences. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars.

Q: What is the typical terrain like for road cycling in the Ayllón region?
A: The Ayllón region, part of the Central System mountain range, offers varied terrain for road cycling. You'll encounter rolling hills, challenging climbs, and scenic routes through ancient forests and dramatic river gorges. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1068237466/ayllon-and-riaza-river-meanders-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ayllón and Riaza River Meanders Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for example, features significant elevation gain and views of impressive gorges.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes near Ayllón?
A: While many routes in Ayllón feature moderate to difficult climbs, there is at least one easy route available. For families, consider routes with less elevation gain and shorter distances. Always check the route details on komoot for specific difficulty ratings and terrain before heading out.

Q: What kind of natural attractions can I expect to see along the road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes around Ayllón offer stunning natural beauty. You can ride through areas near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5438613" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hayedo de Tejera Negra Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, one of Europe's southernmost beech forests, or enjoy dramatic views of the gorges carved by the Riaza River, as seen on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1068237466/ayllon-and-riaza-river-meanders-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ayllón and Riaza River Meanders Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or points of interest along the cycling routes?
A: Yes, several routes incorporate historical landmarks. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24541127" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medieval Gate of Ayllón – San Bartolomé, Campisábalos loop from Ayllón&lt;/a&gt; leads through varied terrain with historical sites. You can also find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4225597" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hontanares Hermitage&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4623981" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor de Riaza&lt;/a&gt; in the wider area.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Ayllón?
A: The region's diverse landscapes, including beech forests, are particularly beautiful in autumn with their vibrant colors. Spring and early summer also offer pleasant temperatures for cycling. Always check the local weather forecast before planning your ride, especially for routes with significant elevation.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available from Ayllón?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Ayllón are designed as loops. Popular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1352539708/ayllon-and-maderuelo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ayllón and Maderuelo Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1068237466/ayllon-and-riaza-river-meanders-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ayllón and Riaza River Meanders Loop&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to start and end in the same location.

Q: What is the average duration for road cycling routes in Ayllón?
A: Route durations vary significantly based on distance and difficulty. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1352539708/ayllon-and-maderuelo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ayllón and Maderuelo Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes approximately 1 hour 55 minutes, while more challenging routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24541145" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Aguisejo River – Alto de la Carrascosa loop from Santa María de Riaza&lt;/a&gt; can take over 5 hours.

Q: Are there options for advanced road cyclists seeking a challenge?
A: Absolutely. The Ayllón region offers several difficult routes with substantial elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24541145" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Aguisejo River – Alto de la Carrascosa loop from Santa María de Riaza&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 116.6 km route with over 1400 meters of ascent, perfect for experienced riders.

Q: Where can I find parking facilities for cyclists in Ayllón?
A: Ayllón, as a cycling-friendly area, generally offers parking options within the town. While specific dedicated cyclist parking facilities are not detailed, public parking areas are typically available. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival for the most convenient spots.

Q: Are there cafes or restaurants along the road cycling routes near Ayllón?
A: Many routes pass through or near small towns and villages where you can find cafes and restaurants. Ayllón itself has amenities, and routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1352539708/ayllon-and-maderuelo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ayllón and Maderuelo Loop&lt;/a&gt; will take you through Maderuelo, offering opportunities for refreshments.

Q: Can I bring my dog on road cycling routes in the Ayllón area?
A: While road cycling routes themselves are generally accessible, regulations for dogs can vary, especially within protected natural areas like the Natural Park of the Hoces of the Riaza River. It's best to check specific park rules regarding pets before planning a route that enters these zones.
tips: On the outskirts of Ayllón, from the main road you can see the ruins of the Old Convent of San Francisco de Ayllón. The old Convent of the Franciscan Conception was founded in 1528.

In this area of the Río Riaza before reaching the town of Maderuelo, we find immense meanders created by the river itself and that even over the years has formed vertical walls that today are cliffs - mowed
Beautiful village that is located in one of the meanders of the Río Riaza and that forms an entrance to the same river. After the town there is a bridge that joins both sides of the Riaza bank.
As you approach the old city from the west and cross the Aguisejo River, the impressive medieval gate in the city wall admires you in all its splendor.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Maderuelo from Ayllón — circular route?
faqs.answer: This road cycling route is categorized as moderate. While much of it offers tranquil riding on empty single-lane roads, be prepared for challenging sections, including a particularly steep and winding paved stretch nicknamed "the Great Wall of China" that will test your endurance.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the towns of Ayllón or Maderuelo to begin your ride. As it's a circular route, you'll finish back where you started.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route features diverse terrain, including empty single-lane roads and winding mountain passes. You'll cycle through a region characterized by mountain peaks, silent canyons, flowery meadows, and crimped fields. A significant portion traverses the Natural Park of the Hoces del Río Riaza, offering impressive natural landscapes.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Maderuelo from Ayllón — circular route?
faqs.answer: Key highlights include the Old Convent of San Francisco de Ayllón, the scenic Meanders of the Riaza River, the charming settlement of Maderuelo, and the historic Medieval Gate of Ayllón. The route also passes through the Natural Park of the Hoces del Río Riaza.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the region's characteristics, spring and autumn are generally ideal for cycling this route. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery, avoiding the intense heat of summer or the colder conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Maderuelo from Ayllón — circular route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, so while dogs are generally not suitable companions for the entire ride on a racebike, if you are considering bringing a dog, ensure they are safely secured in a bike trailer or carrier. Always check local regulations, especially within the Natural Park of the Hoces del Río Riaza, regarding pets.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are generally required to cycle this public road route. However, always respect local regulations, especially when passing through protected areas like the Natural Park of the Hoces del Río Riaza.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for refreshments or accommodation along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the towns of Ayllón and Maderuelo, which are part of the route, offer options for cafes, pubs, and accommodation. These are good places to refuel and rest.

faqs.question: What is 'the Great Wall of China' section mentioned for this route?
faqs.answer: "The Great Wall of China" refers to a particularly challenging and memorable section of the route. It's known for its terrifyingly steep, paved zig-zags, which will demand significant effort and endurance from cyclists.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of other official trails, including the Ruta del Sureste, Camino Natural de la Cañada Real Soriana Occidental, and the Embalse de Linares del Arroyo.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and the presence of challenging sections like "the Great Wall of China," this route is generally not recommended for absolute beginners. Cyclists should have some experience with road cycling and be prepared for significant climbs.

waypoints.name: Old Convent of San Francisco de Ayllón
